summary: DHH observes an annoying browser rendering issue where CSS-styled pages briefly flash their unstyled HTML before the stylesheet loads and applies. He acknowledges that CSS is meant to be optional and that browsers rendering content before all resources load is by design, but argues the flash of unstyled content is a poor user experience. He notes that Opera 6.0 handles this correctly, implying other browsers should follow suit.
key_points: CSS-heavy page designs suffer from a flash of unstyled content (FOUC) before stylesheets load
The browser behavior of rendering HTML before all resources load, while well-intentioned, creates a jarring visual experience
Opera 6.0 solved this problem, suggesting it was a solvable browser implementation issue rather than an inherent limitation
money_quotes: Why oh why must the entirely CSS'ed page designs render a non-styled version of the page half a second before showing the real deal?
There must be a better way that includes not flashing a non-styled page. Or at least there ought to be one.
key_insight: The flash of unstyled content in early CSS adoption revealed a tension between progressive rendering principles and actual user experience that browsers needed to resolve.
